content.text: What it is

Use-case segmentation divides an enterprise's AI initiative inventory into tiers based on two primary dimensions: business value (the magnitude of impact on revenue, cost, quality, or risk) and technical feasibility (the maturity of available AI capabilities, data availability, and implementation complexity). The resulting 2x2 matrix produces four portfolio segments — Invest (high value, high feasibility), Incubate (lower value, high feasibility), Research (high value, low feasibility), and Deprioritize (low value, low feasibility) — each with a distinct funding and governance posture.
Why it matters

Without portfolio segmentation, AI programs treat all use cases as roughly equivalent investments: each gets a project plan, a delivery team, and budget. In practice, the expected return per dollar invested varies by orders of magnitude across use cases, and the delivery risk varies by the same amount. A use case with a proven AI pattern (document summarization, classification) and a clean data source is a fundamentally different investment than a use case requiring novel agent architecture and data engineering from scratch. Treating them identically creates misallocated talent, over-investment in low-return initiatives, and under-investment in the high-return ones that could define the program's strategic impact.
Benefits
Concentrates talent on highest-return initiatives: Invest tier use cases get the senior engineering and product resources; Incubate tier uses more junior delivery.
Provides a defensible basis for deprioritization: saying 'this use case scores low on both dimensions' is a more sustainable position than 'we ran out of budget'.
Creates different governance tracks by tier: Invest uses full model risk review; Incubate uses a lighter process; Research uses innovation governance rather than delivery governance.

Enables honest stakeholder communication: a use case in the Research tier can be pursued with curiosity rather than committed delivery timelines, which reduces the political pressure that causes teams to overpromise on feasibility.
Simplifies portfolio rebalancing: when new use cases are proposed, scoring them against the same dimensions produces a defensible placement decision that can be compared to existing initiatives.
Trade-offs and limitations
Scoring is subjective: value and feasibility scores require judgment calls; the process must include stakeholder calibration to prevent gaming or bias toward familiar use cases.
Quadrant boundaries are not sharp: a use case at the center of the matrix is not clearly in any quadrant; forced binary assignments at the boundary cause unnecessary conflict.
Feasibility changes over time: a use case that was Research tier in 2022 (novel capability) may be Invest tier in 2025 as the underlying models mature; the portfolio must be re-scored periodically.

Value estimates are uncertain: for transformational use cases, the most interesting value may be speculative; segmentation should flag high-uncertainty estimates rather than treating them as equivalent to well-evidenced estimates.
How it works

Business value scoring typically combines three sub-dimensions: financial impact (annualized cost reduction plus revenue lift in dollars), strategic alignment (how directly the use case advances stated strategic priorities), and risk reduction (reduction in operational, regulatory, or reputational risk). Each sub-dimension is scored on a 1-5 scale and weighted: financial impact typically receives the highest weight (50%), strategic alignment receives 30%, and risk reduction receives 20%.

Technical feasibility scoring combines data readiness (is the required training and inference data available, clean, and accessible?), model maturity (does a production-ready model or agent pattern exist for this use case, or does it require significant research?), and integration complexity (how many systems must be integrated, and what is the change management burden?). As with value, each sub-dimension is scored 1-5 and weighted.

The resulting scores place each use case in a 5x5 space, which is then divided into quadrants by setting thresholds. Most programs set the threshold at 3.0 for both dimensions, but the threshold can be adjusted to manage portfolio size — setting a higher threshold tightens the Invest tier, which is appropriate when delivery capacity is constrained.
Architecture

Use cases are plotted by their value score (x-axis) and feasibility score (y-axis). Bubble size represents planned investment. Green (Invest): full delivery commitment. Blue (Incubate): lighter delivery with learning objectives. Yellow (Research): time-boxed investigation. Grey (Deprioritize): no active investment.
When to use it

Portfolio segmentation should be conducted at the start of the program (to prioritize the initial use case portfolio) and repeated at each annual planning cycle (to re-score existing use cases given new capability availability and updated value evidence). It is also triggered when the portfolio size grows beyond the program's delivery capacity, creating pressure to prioritize. Use cases proposed by business units should be scored before they are added to the portfolio, not after — retrospective scoring rationalizes decisions rather than informing them.
How to implement

Define and agree on scoring criteria: convene a cross-functional team (Finance, Engineering, Business, Risk) to agree on the value and feasibility sub-dimensions and weights before scoring any use case.

Build the scoring rubric: for each sub-dimension, write explicit anchors for scores 1, 3, and 5 (e.g., financial impact: 1 = less than $100K annual, 3 = $500K-$2M annual, 5 = greater than $5M annual). Explicit anchors prevent calibration drift.

Score independently, then calibrate: have three to four people score each use case independently, then reconcile the scores in a structured discussion. Disagreement surfaces assumptions that must be made explicit.

Plot and review: visualize the portfolio in the 2x2 matrix. The distribution itself is informative — a portfolio with everything in the Research quadrant indicates a program that has not yet identified deployable value; a portfolio with everything in Incubate indicates risk aversion.

Assign portfolio actions: for each quadrant, define the default action — Invest: full project resourcing; Incubate: 20% capacity, time-boxed; Research: innovation pod, 3-month time box; Deprioritize: no active work, archive.
Re-score annually: technology capability changes rapidly; a feasibility score from 18 months ago may be significantly understated.
Examples

Portfolio tier management requires an audit log of tier transitions. When a use case moves from Research to Invest, the reason and evidence should be recorded. This creates accountability and builds institutional knowledge about what actually changes tier placement:

# Portfolio tier transition tracker
# Records when use cases change tiers and why; maintains audit trail

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class TierTransition:
    use_case: str
    from_tier: str
    to_tier: str
    date: date
    reason: str
    evidence: str  # what changed to justify the move

@dataclass
class PortfolioAuditLog:
    transitions: list = field(default_factory=list)

    def record(self, uc: str, from_tier: str, to_tier: str,
               reason: str, evidence: str, dt: date = None):
        self.transitions.append(TierTransition(
            use_case=uc, from_tier=from_tier, to_tier=to_tier,
            date=dt or date.today(), reason=reason, evidence=evidence
        ))

    def tier_history(self, use_case: str) -> list:
        return [t for t in self.transitions if t.use_case == use_case]

log = PortfolioAuditLog()
log.record(
    "Predictive maintenance (novel sensor data)",
    from_tier="RESEARCH", to_tier="INVEST",
    reason="Data readiness improved: sensor telemetry pipeline now operational",
    evidence="Data quality score increased from 2.1 to 4.3 after sensor platform upgrade",
    dt=date(2025, 6, 1)
)
log.record(
    "Generic chatbot FAQ",
    from_tier="INCUBATE", to_tier="DEPRIORITIZE",
    reason="Low adoption after 3-month incubation: 8% utilization vs 60% target",
    evidence="Adoption metrics from analytics dashboard, Q1 2025",
    dt=date(2025, 4, 15)
)

for t in log.transitions:
    print(f"{t.date}: {t.use_case}")
    print(f"  {t.from_tier} -> {t.to_tier}: {t.reason}")
Real-world example

A large insurance company conducted a portfolio segmentation exercise with 32 proposed AI use cases across six business units. Before segmentation, the program office had informally committed to delivering all 32 within 24 months — a commitment that required more than three times the available delivery capacity. The segmentation exercise (conducted over two full-day workshops with cross-functional participation) produced 8 Invest, 10 Incubate, 9 Research, and 5 Deprioritize assignments.

The 5 Deprioritize assignments were the most valuable outcome of the exercise: each had been advocated for by a specific business unit, and without the segmentation framework, the program office would have had no principled basis for declining them. With the scoring rubric, the conversation shifted from 'we won't fund your use case' to 'this use case scores 1.8 on value and 2.1 on feasibility — here is the evidence behind those scores and here is what would need to change to move it into the Incubate tier.' Two of the five were resubmitted with improved data readiness plans and moved to Incubate within 6 months.
Common mistakes
Scoring without explicit anchors: without written rubric anchors, scores reflect the scorer's intuition rather than a consistent standard; calibration gaps lead to results that stakeholders distrust.

Letting the most vocal advocate win: segmentation discussions often conclude by converging on the position of the most senior person in the room rather than on the evidence; structured scoring with independent initial assessments prevents this.

Not re-scoring as technology changes: a feasibility score from 18 months ago may significantly understate current capability; Research tier use cases should be re-evaluated quarterly as the underlying model capabilities evolve.
Treating tiers as permanent: a use case's tier should change in response to new evidence; a Research use case that achieves a data breakthrough should be re-scored and potentially move to Invest.

Segmenting infrastructure alongside use cases: infrastructure capabilities (LLM gateway, MLOps) are not use cases and should not appear in the segmentation matrix; they are prerequisites, not portfolio items.
Best practices
Run scoring workshops with cross-functional participants: value scoring without Finance and business unit leads lacks credibility; feasibility scoring without Engineering and Data lacks accuracy.
Publish the full scoring rubric alongside results: transparency about how scores were derived makes the segmentation defensible to stakeholders whose use cases were deprioritized.

Review the portfolio distribution: a healthy portfolio has items in all four quadrants; a portfolio with nothing in Research is risk-averse; a portfolio with nothing in Invest lacks strategic ambition.
Set tier-specific investment limits: Invest uses standard delivery funding; Incubate is capped at 20% of a standard delivery budget; Research is time-boxed to 90 days and a small fixed budget.

Track tier transitions: maintain a log of use cases that moved between tiers and why; this builds institutional knowledge about what changes tier placement and helps teams frame improvement proposals.
Performance and scalability

Portfolio segmentation performance is measured by the accuracy of tier assignments over time: what percentage of Invest tier use cases delivered their projected value? What percentage of Research tier use cases generated learnings that accelerated downstream Invest tier delivery? These retrospective accuracy rates calibrate the scoring rubric for future cycles. A well-calibrated rubric should produce 80%+ value delivery rate from Invest tier use cases and 70%+ useful learning rate from Research tier investments.

Re-score Research tier quarterly; full portfolio annually

Security and reliability

Portfolio tier has security implications: Invest tier use cases require the full security review process (data classification, access control design, prompt injection testing, audit logging), while Incubate tier may use a lighter security review for internal-only, low-sensitivity use cases. Research tier use cases operate in sandboxed environments with no production data access — the tier designation is itself a security control. Define security requirements by tier in the governance framework before the portfolio is populated.
Key takeaways
Score use cases on two dimensions — business value and technical feasibility — and use the scores to assign portfolio tiers that determine governance, resourcing, and timeline.
Write explicit rubric anchors for each sub-dimension before scoring: without anchors, scores reflect intuition rather than evidence and are not defensible.
Run scoring with cross-functional teams: value scores without Finance and feasibility scores without Engineering are not credible.
Re-score the portfolio annually and Research tier use cases quarterly: technology capability changes make yesterday's Research tier item today's Invest tier item.
Publish the full rubric and scores: transparency is what makes deprioritization decisions sustainable rather than politically contentious.
Infrastructure capabilities are not portfolio use cases — they are prerequisites and should be planned separately.
